topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone screen, a familiar chime that usually signaled a new assignment, but this time, it was different. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My breath hitched. I fumbled with my phone, my fingers suddenly clumsy, and tapped the message. It was a photo—a candid shot of me from a class presentation, taken from a slightly different angle than the one he usually captured. Below it, a short, cryptic comment: "Good work, Alex." A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I quickly minimized the app, my heart thumping an uneven rhythm against my ribs. I started replaying the last few weeks, scrutinizing every interaction, searching for some hidden meaning in his usual, reserved demeanor. The silence of my room felt suddenly oppressive, amplifying the frantic buzzing inside my head.

[story 2]

I was scrolling through my feed during my lunch break, mindlessly flicking through posts from friends, when I saw it. A picture of me, clearly taken by someone else, but the context—the location, the time stamp—was undeniably linked to a recent school event. Then, a notification. It was a direct message from Professor Evans. His name, usually a distant figure in lecture halls, staring back at me from my social media timeline. My hand froze halfway to closing the app. A flush crept up my neck, warm and undeniable. I swallowed hard, tasting metal in my mouth. I tried to focus on my sandwich, but the edges of my vision seemed to blur. Every movement felt exaggerated, slow, as if I were moving underwater. I kept glancing toward the door, half-expecting him to materialize.

[story 3]

It happened during late-night studying. I was just about to log off when my feed updated. A picture of me, taken moments before I left the library, was there, accompanied by a message from Ms. Davies. It wasn't a casual 'nice work' remark; it was a direct acknowledgment, a personal tag. My palms began to sweat, slick against the cool surface of my desk. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. My mind raced, constructing scenarios that felt increasingly improbable and unsettling. Why would she be sharing these things? The air in the room seemed to thin out, leaving me feeling exposed and terribly scrutinized. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between my desk and the window, unable to settle.
